digest.top_line: FIRESTORM: dose-escalated postop RT (BED ≥79.2Gy) lifts 3-yr PFS to 86.4% vs 55.6% in high-risk meningioma (MVA HR 0.40), no OS gain.
digest.tldr: Three risk-calibrated RT studies, split between escalation and omission. FIRESTORM escalates high-risk meningioma dose (3-yr PFS 86.4 vs 55.6%, HR 0.40) at 2.5x radionecrosis and no OS gain; RTOG 0539 sets mature 10-yr risk-adapted benchmarks. RAPCHEM de-escalates regional nodal RT after NAC (2.9% 10-yr LRR), uncontrolled.

sites.intro: Lone breast entry is a de-escalation signal: regional nodal RT omitted by nodal response after NAC, but uncontrolled and pending the randomized NSABP test (~3yr out).

studies.name: RAPCHEM
studies.tldr: 10-yr locoregional recurrence 2.9% (24/838) with risk-adapted RT after neoadjuvant chemo; regional nodes omitted in pts with 1-3 residual nodes.
details: 🔍 Prospective cohort (BOOG 2010-03), N=848, 17 Dutch centers 2011-2015; RT tailored to nodal response after neoadjuvant chemo + surgery
🔍 Eligibility: breast tumor <5cm with 1-3 clinically positive nodes (cN1); most underwent axillary lymph node dissection

details.text: 📊 10-yr locoregional recurrence by risk stratum (RT received)

rows: Breast/chest wall RT, no regional nodes

details: 📊 Overall 10-yr locoregional recurrence 24/838 (2.9%), all without distant disease
⚠️ No comparator: study did not compare with vs without RT, so de-escalation non-inferiority is unproven
⚠️ Most had ALND (now less common); limits transfer to SLNB-era axillary de-escalation
🔗 NSABP randomized trial (NCT01872975) is the pending definitive test of regional nodal RT after NAC; results expected ~3yr per authors

verdict.rationale: Prospective risk-stratified cohort, no randomized with/without-RT comparator; can't establish de-escalation non-inferiority. Authors defer to NSABP RCT (NCT01872975).
verdict.audience: cT<5cm, cN1 (1-3 nodes) breast cancer post-neoadjuvant chemo + surgery, mostly…

studies.significance: Risk-adapted by post-chemo nodal response, not baseline stage: pts with 1-3 residual nodes (ypN1) got breast/chest-wall RT with regional nodes OMITTED and reached 3.2% 10-yr LRR; ypN0 post-mastectomy pts received no RT (within the 2.4% low-risk stratum). Moves the RNI-omission decision, though most had ALND.
studies.monday_clinic: In cN1 breast cancer downstaged by neoadjuvant chemo (ypN0 to ypN1) and staged with ALND, the low 10-yr LRR supports risk-adapted regional-nodal RT omission; it does not extend to SLNB-only axillae, which the randomized NSABP trial (NCT01872975) will test.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Prospective multicenter cohort (**BOOG 2010-03**), **N=848** across **17 Dutch centers** (2011-2015), RT tailored to post-neoadjuvant nodal response. **Not randomized**; no with/without-RT comparator. 10-yr follow-up.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Breast tumor **under 5 cm** with **1-3 clinically involved nodes** (cN1), treated with **neoadjuvant chemotherapy then surgery**. **Most underwent axillary lymph node dissection**, a procedure now used less often.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: Risk-adapted by nodal response: **no residual nodes → breast RT after BCS, no RT after mastectomy**; **1-3 nodes → chest-wall/breast RT with regional nodes OMITTED**; **≥4 nodes → breast + regional nodal irradiation**.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: **10-yr locoregional recurrence** (in-breast, chest wall, or nodal without distant disease), reported overall and by risk stratum.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Overall LRR **24/838 (2.9%)**, low and consistent across all three strata (per-stratum breakdown in table above).

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: The randomized test of regional nodal RT after NAC is the **NSABP trial (NCT01872975)**; authors expect results in ~3 years and defer the final conclusion to it.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents cT<5cm, cN1 (1-3 nodes) breast cancer downstaged with neoadjuvant chemotherapy and staged mostly by ALND. Does not represent SLNB-only axillary staging or nodal disease outside the studied strata.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: No randomized comparator, so RT de-escalation safety is observed, not proven. Most had ALND, limiting transfer to current sentinel-node practice; the NSABP RCT will provide the definitive comparison.

open_questions: Is tailored RT de-escalation non-inferior without a randomized comparator?
Generalizability to current SLNB-based axillary staging

related_trials.brief_summary:
T-REX is a randomized multicenter, non-inferiority trial.

Aim: To evaluate whether regional radiotherapy may safely be omitted in clinically node negative breast cancer patients with one or two sentinel node macrometastases and an estrogen receptor positive, HER2-negative tumor. Leading to an improved quality of life and reduced side effects, without worsening recurrence free survival at five years.

Intervention: Patients will be randomized to locoregional radiotherapy towards the…

The main questions it aims to answer…

related_trials.answers_question: Generalizability to current SLNB-based axillary staging

sites.intro: Both entries are postop meningioma RT: FIRESTORM tests dose escalation above the ~60Gy standard that RTOG 0539 benchmarks; neither is randomized.

studies.name: RTOG 0539
studies.tldr: 10-yr PFS/OS 85/94% low-risk (obs), 72/85% intermediate (54Gy), 43/51% high-risk (60Gy); mature risk-adapted RT benchmarks.
details: 🔍 Phase 2 prospective risk-adapted RT strategy (NRG/RTOG); 165 treated of 244 consented; centrally-confirmed pathology; NCT00895622

subdetails: Low (n=60): grade 1 post-GTR/STR → observed, no RT
Intermediate (n=52): recurrent grade 1 or new grade 2 post-GTR → 54 Gy
High (n=53): new grade 2 post-STR, grade 3, or recurrent grade 2/3 → 60 Gy

details.text: 🔍 Adjuvant EBRT, both cohorts 30 fractions conventional (54 Gy intermediate, 60 Gy high-risk)

details: 🔍 Median f/u 12.1/12.0/11.1 yrs; median PFS not reached for low- and intermediate-risk cohorts
🔗 Intermediate indication (grade 2 post-GTR → 54 Gy) is exactly what ROAM/EORTC-1308 randomizes vs observation
⚠️ Non-randomized, one cohort per risk tier; no internal observation control for irradiated groups, so RT benefit vs natural history is unproven
⚠️ Long-term analysis of a previously-reported 3-yr PFS primary; descriptive benchmarks, not a fresh hypothesis test

verdict.rationale: Non-randomized, one cohort per risk tier, no internal control vs observation; mature 12-yr data validating the guideline risk-adapted RT framework (54/60 Gy) rather than establishing new benefit.
verdict.audience: Resected WHO grade 1-3 meningioma, risk-stratified by grade + resection

studies.significance: The intermediate cohort (grade 2 post-GTR, 54 Gy) reaching 72.2% 10-yr PFS is the exact benchmark ROAM/EORTC-1308 tests against observation, so this validates the dose but not the indication. The 42.5% high-risk PFS at 60 Gy marks conventional-dose RT's ceiling and motivates escalation for grade 3 / recurrent disease.
studies.monday_clinic: In newly-diagnosed WHO grade 1 meningioma after gross or subtotal resection, this supports observation over adjuvant RT (85.2% 10-yr PFS, 94.1% OS unirradiated); it does not resolve whether grade 2 tumors after GTR benefit from the 54 Gy given here.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: Prospective **phase 2, non-randomized** risk-adapted RT strategy (NRG/RTOG). 244 consented, **165 treated** across 3 risk cohorts with central pathology confirmation. Median follow-up 11-12 yrs.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Adults, Zubrod 0-1, histologically-confirmed unifocal **WHO grade 1-3 meningioma**, any resection extent. Stratified by grade, GTR vs STR, and recurrence status.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: Adjuvant EBRT, 30 fractions: **54 Gy** intermediate-risk, **60 Gy** high-risk. Low-risk observed, no RT.

analysis_sections.label: Endpoints
analysis_sections.body: Original primary was **3-yr PFS** (previously reported). This mature analysis reports 10-yr PFS, OS, and cumulative incidence of progression by cohort.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: 10-yr PFS falls sharply by risk tier (see table). Median PFS not reached for low- and intermediate-risk cohorts.

analysis_sections.label: Safety
analysis_sections.body: **Grade 3+ RT-attributed toxicity** 9.6% (5/52) intermediate, 15.1% (8/53) high-risk. No RT toxicity in the observed low-risk cohort.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: The grade-2-post-GTR → 54 Gy indication is precisely what **ROAM/EORTC-1308** randomizes vs observation; a single-arm cohort here cannot prove RT adds benefit.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents resected WHO grade 1-3 meningioma risk-stratified by grade, resection extent, and recurrence. Does not represent unresected, multifocal, or biopsy-only meningioma.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: **Non-randomized**, one cohort per risk tier, no internal observation control for irradiated groups. Long-term follow-up of an already-reported primary, not a new hypothesis test.

open_questions: Does adjuvant RT beat observation for grade 2 meningioma post-GTR?
Dose escalation above 60 Gy for high-risk / grade 3 meningioma?

related_trials.answers_question: Dose escalation above 60 Gy for high-risk / grade 3 meningioma?

studies.name: FIRESTORM
studies.tldr: 3-yr PFS 86.4% vs 55.6% with dose-escalated (BED ≥79.2Gy) vs standard-dose postop RT for high-risk meningioma; MVA HR 0.40, no OS benefit.

details.text: 📊 PFS by dose group (DE-RT ≥79.2 Gy BED vs SD-RT)

details: 🔍 IPD meta-analysis, 7 institutions, N=248 (59 DE-RT, 189 SD-RT); non-randomized, propensity IPTW-adjusted

details.text: 🔍 DE-RT = BED ≥79.2 Gy (66 Gy/33 fx eq); SD-RT = 59.4 Gy/33 fx or 60 Gy/30 fx

details: 📊 Largest benefit in Simpson grade 4-5: 5-yr PFS 63.3% vs 38.4%, MVA HR 0.55 (0.36-0.84), p=.006
⚠️ Non-randomized dose comparison: DE-RT allocation prone to confounding by indication despite IPTW

details.text: 🔗 Escalates beyond the ~60 Gy postop standard (RTOG 0539 high-risk arm, EORTC 22042); PFS-only, no randomized dose-escalation data yet

verdict.rationale: Retrospective pooled cohorts; non-randomized dose comparison prone to confounding by indication that propensity/IPTW can't fully adjust. OS benefit not established.
verdict.audience: High-risk meningioma: WHO grade 2 after STR or grade 3, postop RT

studies.significance: The PFS benefit is largest where residual disease is greatest: Simpson grade 4-5 resections showed 5-yr PFS 63.3% vs 38.4% (HR 0.55), and DE-RT held after excluding hypofractionated and prior-RT cases (HR 0.37). It moves the dose-escalation-above-60Gy decision, but OS was unchanged (HR 0.66, ns).
studies.monday_clinic: For a WHO grade 2 meningioma with residual after subtotal resection (or a grade 3) headed to postoperative RT, the PFS signal favors dose escalation above 60Gy; it does not extend to an OS gain or to gross-totally-resected grade 1 disease.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: **IPD meta-analysis** pooling 7 institutions, N=248 high-risk meningioma (**59 DE-RT, 189 SD-RT**). Non-randomized; DE-RT vs SD-RT compared by Cox MVA and propensity **IPTW**.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: **188 (75.8%) WHO grade 2**, remainder grade 3; **103 (41.5%) recurrent**. Subtotal resection in **182/248 (75.2%)**.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: **DE-RT = BED ≥79.2 Gy** (66 Gy/33 fx equivalent); **SD-RT = 59.4 Gy/33 fx or 60 Gy/30 fx**. A subset of DE-RT used mixed photon/carbon (no PFS difference vs photon-alone, p=.34).

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: **OS not improved** by DE-RT: 5-yr 83.8% vs 68.4% (UVA p=.056); MVA HR 0.66 (0.33-1.34), p=.27; IPTW HR 0.77, p=.42.

analysis_sections.label: Safety
analysis_sections.body: Any-grade radionecrosis higher with DE-RT (**33.9% vs 13.2%, p=.001**) but **grade 3+ RN similar** (5.1% vs 3.2%). Cohort-wide 18.1% any-grade RN, including 2 grade 5 events.

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: Standard postoperative dose in prospective high-risk meningioma series (RTOG 0539 high-risk arm, EORTC 22042) sits at ~60 Gy; this analysis argues escalation past that improves **PFS but not OS**.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents high-risk meningioma (WHO grade 2 after subtotal resection, or grade 3) receiving postoperative fractionated RT. Does not represent gross-totally-resected grade 1 disease or patients managed with upfront radiosurgery.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Non-randomized: DE-RT allocation **confounded by indication**, only partly addressed by IPTW. Modest DE-RT n=59; PFS benefit without OS gain; higher any-grade radionecrosis.

analysis_sections.label: Discussion
analysis_sections.body: Largest PFS gain in **Simpson grade 4-5 resections** (5-yr 63.3% vs 38.4%, HR 0.55) fits a residual-disease dose-response, and benefit persisted after excluding hypofractionated and prior-RT cases (HR 0.37). Awaits randomized confirmation.

open_questions: Randomized confirmation of dose escalation for high-risk meningioma
Whether PFS gain translates to an OS benefit
Optimal dose, fractionation, and BED threshold

papers.title: Breast Cancer Recurrence Remains Low—Even After 10 Years—With Radiotherapy Tailored to Patient’s Individual Risk
papers.abstract: “The results of our study show that tailoring the extent of radiotherapy according to how well the chemotherapy has worked to treat cancer in the lymph nodes leads to very low and reassuring recurrenc...
papers.note: Breast Cancer Recurrence Remains Low Even After 10 Years With Radiotherapy Tailored to Patient’s Individual Risk - The ASCO Post

papers.title: Long-Term Analysis of NRG Oncology RTOG 0539: A Phase II Trial of Observation for Low-Risk Meningioma and Radiotherapy for Intermediate- and High-Risk Meningioma
papers.abstract: NRG Oncology RTOG 0539 was a prospective phase II trial of risk-adapted radiotherapy for patients with WHO grade 1-3 meningioma. Low-risk (group 1, n = 60) was defined as a grade 1 tumor after gross total resection or subtotal resection (GTR/STR) and prospectively monitored. Intermediate-risk (group 2, n = 52) was defined as recurrent grade 1 or newly diagnosed grade 2 tumor after GTR and treated with radiotherapy (54 Gy). High-risk (group 3, n = 53) included a newly diagnosed grade 2 tumor after STR, newly diagnosed grade 3 tumor, or recurrent grade 2 or 3 tumor and treated with radiotherapy (60 Gy). Progression-free survival (PFS) and overall survival (OS) were estimated using the Kaplan-Meier method. The median follow-up times for the low-, intermediate-, and high-risk cohorts were 12.1, 12.0, and 11.1 years, respectively. The 10-year PFS and OS rates for the low-, intermediate-, and high-risk cohorts were 85.2% and 94.1%, 72.2% and 84.7%, and 42.5% and 51.1%, respectively. Five patients (9.6%) and eight patients (15.1%) had a grade 3+ toxicity attributed to radiotherapy in the intermediate- and high-risk cohorts, respectively. The long-term outcomes using this risk-adapted approach support observation for low-risk patients, inform radiotherapy patient selection and practice standards for intermediate- and high-risk patients, and provide comparative benchmarks for future trials.

papers.title: Improved Progression-Free Survival Following Dose-Escalated Versus Standard-Dose Postoperative Radiation Therapy for High-Risk Meningiomas: An International Multicenter Individual Patient–Level Meta-Analysis (FIRESTORM)
papers.abstract:
PURPOSE: We performed an individual patient-level meta-analysis of high-risk meningiomas to compare the outcomes of dose-escalated radiation therapy (DE-RT) versus standard-dose postoperative radiation therapy (SD-RT).

METHODS AND MATERIALS: A total of 7 institutions participated. DE-RT was defined as treatment with a biologically effective dose of ≥79.2 Gy (equivalent of 66 Gy in 33 fractions). We compared progression-free survival (PFS) with DE-RT versus SD-RT via Kaplan-Meier analysis and log-rank t tests, a Cox proportional hazards multivariable model, and propensity score analyses with inverse probability of treatment weighting (IPTW). We also compared incidences of central nervous system radionecrosis (RN) with DE-RT versus SD-RT.

RESULTS: The analysis included 248 patients with high-risk meningioma (59 received DE-RT and 189 received SD-RT). One hundred and eighty-eight cases (75.8%) were World Health Organization grade 2, and 103 cases (41.5%) were recurrent meningiomas. Extent of resection was subtotal resection in 182 of 248 (75.2%). Three- and 5-year PFS rates were 62.8% (95% CI, 55.8%-69.0%) and 45.0% (95% CI, 37.3%-52.3%), respectively. DE-RT was associated with superior PFS rates (P = .0022), with 3-year (86.4% vs 55.6%) and 5-year (65.8% vs 38.8%) PFS rates favoring DE-RT. On multivariable analysis, DE-RT was associated with superior PFS (hazard ratio, 0.40; 95% CI, 0.24-0.69; P = .001). On IPTW, DE-RT continued to be associated with superior PFS (hazard ratio, 0.45; 95% CI, 0.24-0.83; P = .01). A greater incidence of any grade RN was observed following DE-RT (20 of 59; 33.9%) versus SD-RT (25 of 189; 13.2%) (P = .001) but with similar grade 3 or greater RN events (DE-RT 5.1% vs SD-RT 3.2%).

CONCLUSIONS: DE-RT resulted in superior PFS for patients with high-risk meningiomas over SD-RT without an increase in severe toxicities.
